name: West Side Warner Peak
short_description: BLM rockhounding site in south-central Oregon offering agate, jasper, and obsidian collecting on accessible volcanic terrain with moderate slopes at 5481 feet elevation.
description:
West Side Warner Peak sits within Oregon's high desert volcanic plateau, a productive rockhounding area managed by the Bureau of Land Management. The site features moderate slopes at 5481 feet elevation with good access for recreational collectors seeking agate, jasper, and obsidian specimens. This location exemplifies the volcanic geology of the Basin and Range province, where Miocene-age lava flows and rhyolite deposits created ideal conditions for silica-rich mineral formation.

The terrain here is typical of Lake County's volcanic landscape - scattered basalt outcrops, volcanic ash deposits, and exposed ridges dotted with sagebrush and juniper. You will find agates and jasper nodules weathering out of hillsides and wash bottoms, particularly in areas where volcanic bedrock intersects with drainage channels. Obsidian occurs in glassy chunks and flakes throughout the region, often concentrated near ancient lava flows.

This is an excellent site for rockhounds of moderate experience. The moderate slopes are navigable without technical climbing, and specimens are accessible without extensive excavation. The area remains lightly visited compared to Oregon's famous central Oregon rockhounding sites, offering a quieter collecting experience.

Expect to spend 3 to 5 hours working this area, covering multiple hillsides and washes. Bring plenty of water - the high desert offers little shade and afternoon heat can be intense even in shoulder seasons. The open terrain allows for efficient spotting of specimens, especially after recent rains when weathered material is more visible.
history: West Side Warner Peak sits within the Cascade-Sierra volcanic arc province, where Miocene rhyolitic and basaltic volcanism (roughly 20-15 million years ago) deposited thick sequences of lava flows and pyroclastic materials that host the agate and obsidian deposits found here today. This high desert region was prospected during Oregon's historical mining era, with local rockhounders documenting productive agate and obsidian localities throughout Lake County since the mid-20th century.
tips: Focus your search on hillside slopes where volcanic bedrock is exposed or partially eroded - agates often weather out as rounded nodules on the surface. Scan the bottoms of small drainages and washes where heavier material collects after rainfall; this is where you will find the best specimen concentrations. Look for agate showing translucent color banding in shades of white, gray, red, and brown, and watch for glassy black or mahogany-colored obsidian flakes. Bring a 5-gallon bucket to carry specimens and a hand shovel to check loose volcanic material around hillside seeps, where nodules frequently accumulate.
terrain_notes: Moderate slope volcanic terrain at 5481 feet elevation. Landscape dominated by sagebrush and scattered juniper with exposed basalt and rhyolite outcrops. Walking distances from parking vary from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on access points chosen. Footing is uneven with loose volcanic rock, volcanic cinders, and scattered angular basalt fragments. No formal trails; open cross-country hiking required. Some areas feature scattered boulders and small exposed rock ledges but no major cliff hazards.
best_season: April - May and September - October. Spring offers moderate temperatures and occasional rain that refreshes surface specimen visibility. Fall provides cool mornings and warm afternoons ideal for extended field work. Summer heat is intense at this elevation; July and August are possible but require early morning starts and midday rest. Winter snowfall is light but occasional icing makes footing hazardous.
written_directions: From Lakeview (the largest town in Lake County, approximately 45 miles south), take Highway 395 north toward Burns. After approximately 30 miles, turn east on County Road 3-16 (or local Forest Service road designation - confirm current conditions locally as roads in this remote area shift). Continue east for 8-12 miles on rough dirt road toward Warner Peak. Look for BLM boundary markers and informal pullouts on the west (left) side of the peak. Exact road conditions vary seasonally; contact the Vale District BLM office before traveling to confirm road passability, especially in spring and after storms.
road_conditions: Rough dirt and gravel BLM roads with potential washboard surface. High-clearance vehicle strongly recommended. Road may be impassable during winter or immediately after heavy rain. Confirm current conditions with Vale District BLM office before travel.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along rough dirt access road. No developed parking lot. Limited capacity (2-4 vehicles at small pullouts). Park off roadway to avoid blocking access.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. All collecting must be by hand tools (picks, shovels, hammers). Do not damage living vegetation or create new roads. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection or sales
No motorized excavation equipment
Hand tools only (picks, shovels, hammers permitted)
Do not damage vegetation
Do not create new access roads
Pack out all trash
Resect BLM boundary markers
nearest_services: Lakeview, Oregon (approximately 45 miles south) has gas, food, water, and lodging. Vale, Oregon (approximately 40 miles northwest) offers basic services. Closest ranger station: Vale District BLM Office, Vale OR.
accessibility_notes: Moderate to uneven rocky terrain with loose volcanic material and scattered basalt fragments. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support strongly recommended. Terrain is open cross-country hiking with no developed trails - requires basic route-finding ability. Sloping hillsides require moderate climbing effort. Not suitable for limited mobility or those unable to navigate uneven ground.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find at West Side Warner Peak in Oregon?
faq.answer: West Side Warner Peak offers excellent collecting for agate, jasper, obsidian, and sunstone. Agates here typically display translucent white, gray, red, and brown banding. Black and mahogany obsidian is common and found as glassy flakes and small nodules throughout the volcanic terrain. This is a great location if you want to find multiple gem types in a single trip.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at West Side Warner Peak free or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at West Side Warner Peak is completely free - no permit required. The site is on BLM public land where personal use collecting is allowed without fees under FLPMA regulations. You are limited to 25 pounds per day of collected material. No commercial collection is permitted.

faq.question: How do I get to West Side Warner Peak and what vehicle do I need?
faq.answer: From Lakeview, take Highway 395 north for approximately 30 miles, then turn east on County Road 3-16 toward Warner Peak. Continue 8-12 miles on rough dirt BLM roads to reach the site. A high-clearance vehicle (AWD recommended) is essential due to rough road conditions. The access road may be impassable during winter or after heavy rain - always confirm current conditions with the Vale District BLM office before traveling.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound West Side Warner Peak?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding seasons are April through May and September through October when temperatures are moderate (60-75 degrees F). These months offer ideal collecting conditions without extreme heat or cold. Summer (July-August) is possible but very hot with intense afternoon sun. Avoid winter when occasional snow and ice make footing hazardous. Spring rains often refresh surface specimen visibility, making April-May particularly productive.

faq.question: Is West Side Warner Peak suitable for beginning rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, this is a good beginner and family site. The moderate slope terrain allows children ages 8 and up to participate safely with supervision. Specimens are accessible on hillsides and in washes without requiring advanced techniques or dangerous excavation. The open landscape makes it easy to supervise children. Bring plenty of water and shade - the high desert offers minimal shelter. Dogs are welcome on public BLM land.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at West Side Warner Peak?
faq.answer: Bring a hand shovel or small pick to check loose volcanic material and expose nodules, a small hammer for breaking specimens, and a 5-gallon bucket for carrying your daily 25-pound limit. Wear sturdy hiking boots with ankle support for the rocky terrain. Essential supplies: at least 3 liters of water per person, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), a backpack, and a map. The area is remote with no cell service, so inform someone of your location and expected return time.

meta_title: West Side Warner Peak Rockhounding - Oregon
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Lakeview. Find agate, jasper, and obsidian. No permit needed. 25 lbs/day limit. April-October best.
